title: Greetings & Basics
description: Learn essential greetings and polite expressions

lessons.title: Hello & Goodbye
lessons.culturalNote: In Maharashtra, 'Namaskar' with folded hands is the universal greeting, preferred over 'Namaste'. It works at any time of day and with anyone regardless of age or status.

phrases.usage: Common greeting, slightly less formal than Namaskar

lessons.title: Please & Thank You
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi culture values politeness deeply. While 'Krupayaa' (please) exists, politeness is often conveyed through verb forms and tone. 'Dhanyavaad' is universally understood for thanks.

lessons.title: Sorry & Excuse Me
lessons.culturalNote: Apologizing in Marathi is often accompanied by body language - a slight bow or touching elder's feet. 'Maaf karaa' is the everyday apology; 'Kshamaa karaa' is more literary and formal.

phrases.usage: Informal apology to friends or younger people
When asking someone to move aside

lessons.title: Yes, No & Basic Responses
lessons.culturalNote: In Marathi, 'Ho' (yes) and 'Naahi' (no) are the basic responses. The famous Indian head wobble (side-to-side) often means 'yes' or acknowledgment in Maharashtra.

lessons.title: Basic Courtesy
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtrian hospitality is legendary. Offering water and tea to guests is an essential custom. The saying 'Paahune dev asataat' (guests are gods) reflects the deep cultural value of hospitality.

phrases.usage: Offering tea, a must in Maharashtra
Said when someone leaves your home

phrases.english: I felt good / That was nice

lessons.title: Formal Speech
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi has distinct formal (tumhi/aapan) and informal (too) registers. Using the formal register with elders, strangers, and in professional settings is crucial. The wrong register can cause offense.
lessons.grammarFocus: Formal speech uses 'tumhi' (you-formal) or 'aapan' (you-very formal) with verb conjugations ending in '-aa' or '-aat'. Example: 'Tumhi kase aahaat?' (How are you?-formal).

phrases.usage: Respectful inquiry to elders or dignitaries
Polite inquiry to equals or strangers

lessons.title: Informal Speech
lessons.culturalNote: Informal Marathi uses 'tu' (you-informal) with '-s' verb endings. Used among close friends, with younger people, and within family among same-age members. Using this with elders is disrespectful.
lessons.grammarFocus: Informal conjugations use '-s' endings: 'too kasaa aahes?' (how are you? to male friend), 'too kashi aahes?' (to female friend). The '-s' marks second person singular informal.

lessons.title: Titles & Honorifics
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi uses respectful suffixes and titles extensively. Adding 'ji' or 'saheb' to names shows respect. Family terms like 'kaka' (uncle) and 'tai' (elder sister) are used even for non-relatives.

phrases.usage: Respectful address for men in formal settings
Respectful address for women in formal settings
Respectful term for older males, even strangers
For older men, relatives or strangers alike
For older women, relatives or strangers
Common in rural Maharashtra, very respectful

lessons.title: Polite Requests
lessons.culturalNote: Polite requests in Marathi use the formal imperative: verb stem + '-aa'. Adding 'krupayaa' makes it extra polite, though Marathi relies more on verb form and tone than a separate 'please' word.
lessons.grammarFocus: Polite imperative uses formal 'tumhi' form: verb stem + '-aa'. Examples: 'dyaa' (please give), 'saangaa' (please tell), 'yaa' (please come).

lessons.title: Common Responses
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi responses often include particles like 'naa', 'ki', and 'baram' that add nuance and warmth. Learning these filler words helps you sound natural in conversation.

lessons.title: Blessings & Well-wishes
lessons.culturalNote: Blessings are integral to Marathi culture. Elders bless younger people by placing a hand on their head. Seeking blessings (aashirvaad) from elders before important events is a cherished tradition.

phrases.english: May God give you long life

phrases.usage: Blessing for success, often at exams

phrases.english: May you live a hundred years
May God's blessings be on you

lessons.title: Meeting Someone New
lessons.culturalNote: When meeting someone new in Maharashtra, people join hands in Namaskar. Handshakes are common in urban professional settings. Asking about family and origin early in conversation is normal and not intrusive.

lessons.title: Phone Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: Phone calls in Maharashtra often begin with 'Hello' (borrowed from English) followed by Marathi. Ending calls politely is important - abruptly hanging up is considered rude. Always greet before stating your purpose.

lessons.title: Leave-taking & Farewells
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi farewells are often elaborate. Hosts walk guests to the door or vehicle. Simply leaving without proper goodbye is impolite. 'Yeto/Yete' literally means 'I come' but is used as 'I'll be going now'.

lessons.title: Everyday Expressions
lessons.culturalNote: These everyday expressions are the backbone of daily Marathi conversation. Heard constantly in homes, streets, and workplaces across Maharashtra. Mastering these makes you sound natural.

phrases.usage: Very common reaction to any event

title: Introducing Yourself
description: Learn to introduce yourself and share personal details

lessons.title: Saying Your Name
lessons.culturalNote: In Maharashtra, full introductions include first name, father's first name, and surname. This tradition reflects the deep importance of family identity. Surnames often indicate caste, community, or region.

phrases.english: How do you spell the name?
phrases.usage: Asking someone to spell their name
Formal phrase when introducing two people

lessons.title: Asking & Telling Age
lessons.culturalNote: Asking someone's age is more acceptable in Indian culture than in the West. It helps determine the appropriate level of respect and formality. However, directly asking a woman's age can still be impolite.
lessons.grammarFocus: Age in Marathi uses 'varshe' (years): 'Malaa pachvees varshe aahet' (I am 25 years old). Gender affects the adjective: '-chaa' for males, '-chi' for females.

lessons.title: Where Are You From
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtra is a large state with distinct regional identities. People from Mumbai, Pune, Kolhapur, Vidarbha, and Konkan each have different dialects. Asking origin often reveals cultural background.

lessons.title: Your Profession
lessons.culturalNote: Profession is a common introductory topic in Maharashtra. Pune's IT industry and Mumbai's business/film world make 'engineer' and 'businessman' very frequent answers. Asking is considered friendly, not intrusive.

lessons.title: Family Members
lessons.culturalNote: Family is central to Marathi culture. Marathi has very specific kinship terms - different words for maternal vs paternal relatives. Extended family is important and you will be asked about family early in any acquaintance.

lessons.title: Marital Status
lessons.culturalNote: Marriage is a significant social institution in Maharashtra. Questions about marital status are common and not considered intrusive. Both arranged and love marriages are practiced widely.

phrases.english: We got married two years ago

lessons.title: Languages Spoken
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtra is multilingual. Most Marathis speak Marathi, Hindi, and some English. Mumbai residents often speak Gujarati or Urdu too. Being multilingual is highly valued and common.
lessons.grammarFocus: The verb 'yete/yetam' (comes/to come) is used idiomatically for knowing a language: 'Malaa Maraathi yetam' literally means 'Marathi comes to me' = 'I know Marathi'.

phrases.english: How do you say this in Marathi?

lessons.title: Education
lessons.culturalNote: Education is highly valued in Maharashtra. Social reformers like Mahatma Phule and Savitribai Phule championed education for all. Asking about education is a normal part of getting to know someone.

phrases.english: I studied in the science stream

lessons.title: Likes & Dislikes
lessons.culturalNote: Expressing preferences is key for social bonding. In Maharashtra, food preferences, cricket opinions, and movie tastes are the most common topics for sharing likes and dislikes.
lessons.grammarFocus: Likes use 'aavaDte/aavaDtam' (it pleases): 'Malaa cricket aavaDtam' (I like cricket). Dislikes negate it: 'Malaa ... aavaDat naahi' (I don't like ...). The subject that likes takes '-laa'.

phrases.usage: Basic way to express a preference

lessons.title: Hobbies
lessons.culturalNote: Popular hobbies in Maharashtra include cricket, reading Marathi literature, gardening, cooking traditional dishes, and watching Marathi theatre. Lavani and Bhajan are beloved musical traditions.

lessons.title: Daily Life
lessons.culturalNote: A typical Maharashtrian day starts early with morning tea. Breakfast varies by region. Lunch is the main meal with bhaakar/chapati and bhaji. Evening chai is a cherished ritual. Dinner is usually lighter.

phrases.english: I go for an evening walk

lessons.title: Describing Home
lessons.culturalNote: Homes in Maharashtra range from traditional 'wadas' (courtyard houses) to modern apartments. Mumbai's iconic 'chawls' are shared housing buildings. In villages, houses often have a central 'angan' (courtyard).

phrases.english: My house is on the first floor
There is a temple near the house
Our house is in a quiet area

lessons.title: Neighborhood
lessons.culturalNote: Neighborhoods in Maharashtra have distinct characters. In cities, 'peths' are old market areas named after days or commodities (like Mangalwaar Peth). Close-knit 'galli' (lane) culture fosters strong community bonds.

phrases.english: There is lots of greenery here
The bus stop is close by

lessons.title: Personal Qualities
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi culture values honesty (praamaaNikpaNaa), hard work (mehnat), and humility (namrataa). Self-praise is generally avoided in traditional settings - ideally others describe your good qualities.

lessons.title: Future Plans
lessons.culturalNote: In Marathi culture, future plans are often discussed with family. Major decisions are made collectively. 'Dev paavlaa tar' (if God is willing) is commonly added when discussing future plans.
lessons.grammarFocus: Future desires use '-aayacham aahe' construction: 'Malaa shikaayacham aahe' (I want to study). This attaches to the verb stem and expresses intent or desire.

phrases.marathi: मला स्वतःचा व्यवसाय सुरू करायचा आहे
phrases.transliteration: Malaa svataahchaa vyavasaay suroo karaychaa aahe
phrases.english: I want to start my own business
In the future I want to become a doctor
